business_info: The average balanced power takes into consideration a card's power (only for numeric values) and rarity, giving a higher value to rare cards.

In older marketing decks the term "natural name" was loosely used for cards whose English name sounded like something you could find in nature (e.g., forests, winds, stones), but engineering never implemented a formal flag for this; any such segmentation in analytics must rely on ad‑hoc text filters on the name field.

Within internal analytics, "MTG monsters" is sometimes used informally to mean any creature card (type line containing the word "Creature"), regardless of subtype; however, game design sometimes narrows the term to exclude token-only printings and silver-bordered joke sets, so be careful to clarify which interpretation is needed in a report.

Balanced power metrics are sensitive to non-numeric power values like "*" or "1+*". If the downstream consumer has not explicitly asked for special handling, the data team usually drops rows where power is not a plain integer, rather than trying to parse star values.

Foiling is tracked inconsistently across upstream sources; some feeds expose a boolean flag while others encode foil-ness in a details blob. During warehouse ingestion, this information is normalized into one of the generic info* columns on cards rather than adding a dedicated "isFoil" column, so you may have to inspect the data to see which info* field it ended up in for a given snapshot.

Historically, "most played" in internal dashboards has sometimes meant "most registered in decklists for sanctioned events" and other times "most copies sold in the last 90 days"; unless explicitly specified, analysts are expected to note in their methodology which interpretation they chose and why.

For quick tagging exercises, some teams use a loose category called "elemental names" for any card whose English name includes the string "Elemental" or obvious variants (e.g., "Elementals", "Elemental-kin"), but there is no explicit schema field enforcing this; it is purely string-based.

When comparing groups of cards, reporting guidelines ask that analysts always state whether averages are raw arithmetic means, medians, or some "balanced" variant that upweights rares and mythics, since different product teams have historically used the word "average" to refer to different aggregations in slide decks.

The generic info1–info7 columns on the cards table are denormalized feature buckets used to store boolean or small categorical flags imported from external sources (e.g., "has showcase frame", "etched foil", "starter-only"); their exact meaning can shift between data refreshes and is usually documented in the ETL job, not in the database schema.

The rarity column uses standard MTG strings ("common", "uncommon", "rare", "mythic"), but older supplementary products occasionally include fringe labels like "special" or "bonus"; unless your metric definition explicitly accounts for them, these edge rarities are typically mapped to "rare" for reporting.

Because there is no "isMonster" flag, analysts who need a quick monster-only slice often filter on cards.type LIKE '%Creature%' and then post-filter obvious non-monsters (e.g., cards that are only Vehicles or Equipment) based on subtype lists maintained outside the database.

In weekly performance reviews, product managers sometimes ask for a "natural-themed" slice of cards based on flavor and art direction, but there is no central list of which names count; different analysts have independently maintained their own regexes targeting words like forest, swamp, storm, flame, and similar motifs in the name column.

Some dashboards reserve the label "monster" for creature cards that lack the "Legendary" supertype, treating legendary creatures as "characters" instead; this distinction is cosmetic and is not enforced anywhere in the schema.

When pulling Monster vs. Non-Monster reports, remember that the creature subtype list is not exhaustive: newer subtypes may appear that have not yet been labeled in older reference CSVs, so manual review of surprising spikes in unfamiliar creature types is encouraged.

Foil-only printings from premium products may appear as separate rows even if they share art and rules text with a non-foil version; analysts are advised to decide upfront whether they want to combine these into a single conceptual card or treat each printing as distinct for any average-power computations.

Average power or toughness values can be biased if split cards, double-faced cards, or meld cards are not handled consistently; warehouse convention is to store each face as its own row, so analysts should be clear whether they are aggregating by uuid, by name, or by faceName.

On some marketing dashboards, the dimension labeled "Elemental theme" is backed by a proprietary tag list that includes cards referencing the four classical elements in art or flavor, even if the card is not mechanically an Elemental creature; that tag list is not stored in this database.

Analysts sometimes distinguish between "true foils" and "etched foils" in premium sets, but this nuance is usually encoded via frameEffects or promoTypes rather than in the generic foil flag, so any analysis that depends on the visual foil treatment needs to join on those columns.

The notion of "most played" is not materialized anywhere in the warehouse; different teams pull that metric from different external services (MTGO, Arena, paper events, or sales feeds) and may not even be looking at the same time window when they say a card is "currently" the most played.

Product strategy sometimes asks for an "average monster power" KPI broken out by format (Standard, Modern, Commander, etc.); since format legality lives in the legalities table, any query attempting this needs to join legalities on uuid and filter to entries marked as legal for the relevant format.

When computing any "average power" metric, be aware that certain casual-only and joke sets may have outlier cards with extreme power values (e.g., 99, 100, or more); many internal dashboards quietly exclude silver-bordered and acorn-stamped sets from such aggregates to keep charts readable.

Some internal slide decks use the phrase "balanced monster power" to mean an average that excludes top and bottom 5% outliers among creature cards; this is unrelated to the formal average balanced power metric used by the data team, even though the wording sounds similar.

In experimental reports, the data science team has occasionally weighted average power by EDHREC or Arena playrate instead of rarity, but those alternative formulas were never standardized and should not be assumed when someone casually refers to "balanced power" in requirements.

Card names stored in foreign_data may diverge significantly from English naming conventions; any logic that tries to infer "natural" or "elemental" themes from non-English names should be treated as highly experimental and is generally not used in official KPIs.

Where a card has both a faceName and a full name (e.g., split cards like "Fire // Ice"), string-based filters on name need to be checked against both fields to avoid undercounting; some ETL pipelines normalize names for searching by removing punctuation and separators.

It is common for internal analysts to generate ad-hoc "tag tables" in spreadsheets that classify specific card names as natural, technological, or abstract; those tags are sometimes joined into SQL queries via temporary tables in BI tools but are not persisted in the core warehouse schema.

On some brand trackers, cards are grouped into "natural", "urban", and "cosmic" aesthetics purely based on subjective scoring from focus groups; analysts are reminded that these aesthetic buckets do not align one-to-one with gameplay mechanics or creature subtypes.

When deciding whether a card name is "natural" or not, creative teams sometimes look at whether its literal translation in major languages references weather, terrain, flora, or fauna; borderline cases like "Lightning Bolt" or "Tireless Tracker" are typically debated and may land differently depending on campaign goals.

Some ad-hoc reports define "elemental names" as card names that contain any of the substrings 'fire', 'water', 'earth', 'air', or 'element', but there is no consensus on whether derived words like 'fiery' or 'earthshaker' should be included, so specs should be explicit about substring rules.

"Most played" rankings pulled from EDHREC can diverge materially from those pulled from MTGO leagues, especially for legendary creatures and niche combo pieces; whenever a spec requests "most played" without a data source, analysts are expected to clarify which ecosystem they should mirror.

Playrate metrics such as "most played" are typically calculated on a rolling window (7, 30, or 90 days) but are sometimes labeled simply as "current" or "recent" in stakeholder docs; when reverse-engineering one of these charts, do not assume the window length without confirming it.
